Information agents will proactively monitor conditions and send alerts without user prompts—tracking stock movements, product releases, or athlete announcements and notifying users when criteria are met. These features will launch first for subscribers to Google's AI Pro and Ultra plans.

Booking agents represent an iteration on Google's earlier Duplex technology, which once made phone calls to restaurants on users' behalf. The updated version will search local business websites and call establishments directly to gather pricing and availability data. Antigravity, Google's code generation tool, will create custom interactive visualizations and "mini apps" for complex tasks like diet tracking or relocation planning. All users will see updated Antigravity outputs later this summer, while expanded functionality reaches U.S. subscribers first.

## The Shift Away from Active User Participation

This represents a fundamental departure from Google Search's original model, where users actively selected from ranked web results. The introduction of AI Overviews in 2024 and AI Mode last year already abstracted that process by generating summaries. The new agents go further: users issue initial instructions, then step back entirely while systems navigate the web and external services independently.

The practical effect is that Google Search increasingly serves users answers and completed tasks rather than pathways to information. While Google states it isn't replacing web links, the architecture keeps users within Google's ecosystem rather than directing them to external sites. For enterprise teams relying on web research workflows or third-party data sources, this shift means reduced visibility into where information originates and less direct access to primary sources. Organizations should assess whether agentic search abstracts away quality signals they currently depend on, and whether search results remain auditable for compliance purposes.
items.original_title: Google Search Goes Agentic—and Doesn’t Need You Anymore

items.headline: Karpathy Joins Anthropic's Pre-Training Team as AI Race Shifts to Research Efficiency
items.body:
Andrej Karpathy, a co-founder of OpenAI who later led AI efforts at Tesla, has joined Anthropic to lead work on language model pre-training. The move signals a strategic pivot among frontier AI labs: as compute costs plateau, competitive advantage increasingly depends on research efficiency rather than raw processing power alone.

Karpathy started this week at Anthropic working under pre-training team lead Nick Joseph. According to the company, he will establish a new team focused on using Claude itself to accelerate pre-training research—essentially applying the AI system to improve the process that builds it. Pre-training is the foundational, compute-intensive phase where models acquire core knowledge before fine-tuning for specific tasks.

## Career Arc and Expertise

Karpathy's background straddles theory and practice in ways few researchers can match. At OpenAI, he focused on deep learning and computer vision until 2017. He then spent five years at Tesla leading Full Self-Driving and Autopilot, managing some of the largest-scale neural network training operations outside of pure AI labs. After a brief return to OpenAI in 2023, he left in 2024 to start Eureka Labs, an education-focused AI startup. His hiring at Anthropic comes as he maintains a public presence through his "Neural Networks: Zero to Hero" course and YouTube channel on LLM fundamentals.

Anthropic simultaneously brought on Chris Rohlf, a 20-year cybersecurity veteran from Meta and Yahoo's "The Paranoids" team, to join its frontier red team that stress-tests models against adversarial threats.

## Research Over Infrastructure

The Karpathy hire reflects a deliberate bet by Anthropic that algorithmic innovation and smarter training methodology will outpace competitors' ability to simply scale compute. While OpenAI and Google dominate in raw processing capacity, Anthropic's strategy centers on research quality and efficiency gains—getting better results per unit of compute spent.

This matters because pre-training represents the largest operational expense in building frontier models. A team using Claude to optimize pre-training workflows could compress timelines and reduce costs, directly improving Anthropic's competitive position. For enterprises evaluating AI vendors, this hiring signals where Anthropic believes the technical frontier actually lies: not in who has the most GPUs, but in who can extract maximum capability from the compute they control. That distinction will shape which labs remain viable competitors over the next 24–36 months.
items.original_title: OpenAI co-founder Andrej Karpathy joins Anthropic’s pre-training team
